children.name: Gadus
children.description: Atlantic cod and its Pacific relatives — fish that shaped human history more profoundly than any other marine species. The collapse of the North Atlantic cod fishery is one of the defining ecological disasters of the 20th century: what was described as an 'inexhaustible' resource by John Cabot in 1497 was reduced to less than 1% of its 1950s population by 1992, when Canada imposed a total moratorium. The Baltic cod (torsk) is in similar crisis — it is currently in two populations, the eastern and western Baltic stock, and the eastern stock has collapsed due to a combination of overfishing, eutrophication (low oxygen in deep water), warming, and reduced salinity following changing precipitation patterns. Cod is still Sweden's most important marine food fish culturally, with lutefisk (lye-treated dried cod) being a traditional Christmas dish.

children.name: Gadus morhua
children.description: The fish that drove European colonisation of North America — the Grand Banks cod were the reason for Cabot's voyage. Baltic cod (torsk) is genetically distinct from North Sea cod. Eastern Baltic stock critically overfished; EU fishing quotas reduced to near-zero. Its recovery is one of European marine conservation's greatest challenges.

children.name: Gadus chalcogrammus
children.description: The world's largest single-species food fishery by volume — over 3 million tonnes caught annually in the Bering Sea. The fish in McDonald's Filet-O-Fish and most supermarket fish fingers. Arguably the most important fish in the global food system.

children.name: Melanogrammus
children.description: Haddock — a close relative of cod, distinguished by a black lateral line and a dark 'thumbprint' above the pectoral fin (according to legend, the mark left by Saint Peter when he plucked a coin from its mouth). Haddock prefers slightly deeper, colder water than cod and is found across the North Sea, Norwegian Sea, and into the North Atlantic. It is a superb food fish with white, flaky flesh — smoked haddock ('Finnan haddie') and haddock in traditional fish and chips are beloved British preparations. Unlike cod, the haddock population has shown genuine recovery in North Sea waters following improved management.

children.name: Melanogrammus aeglefinus
children.description: North Sea haddock populations have recovered well under sustainable fishing quotas. Feeds primarily on echinoderms, polychaetes, and bivalves on the seabed. More cold-tolerant than cod. Smoked haddock is a staple of British and Scandinavian cuisine.

children.name: Merlangius
children.description: Whiting — a slender, swift-swimming gadid of shallow coastal waters and the continental shelf from Iceland to the Mediterranean. Whiting is an important predator of small fish and invertebrates in the North Sea ecosystem and an important food source for seabirds, seals, and larger fish. It is the most cold-tolerant gadid, entering extremely shallow, inshore waters in winter that cod and haddock avoid. In Sweden and the broader North Sea, whiting are caught as bycatch rather than targeted species. Their extremely soft flesh deteriorates quickly, making fresh whiting challenging to market — much is processed into fishmeal.

children.name: Merlangius merlangus
children.description: Extremely abundant in the North Sea and Skagerrak, which form Sweden's western maritime border. An important food source for seabirds and larger fish. Soft flesh decomposes rapidly — must be consumed very fresh. Often bycatch in North Sea trawl fisheries.
